evals.prompt: Use the explain-code skill to walk me through the business logic of the calculateProratedCharge method in src/billing/SubscriptionService.java. The part where it slices the subscription period into segments makes my head spin.
evals.expected_output: A developer-facing explanation: makes clear that when a subscription changes mid-cycle, this method slices the billing period into segments by the 'plan validity window' and only charges for the segments not covered by the active plan; uses a timeline/diagram to mark the key points (activation, subscription start, subscription end, deactivation); walks one example with concrete dates (including cases where activation precedes subscription start, and where multiple consecutive plans are chained); and points out the early-return branch for 'no charge required', any side effects, the string date parsing, and edge cases like plusDays(1).
expectations: Produces at least one diagram (saved as an .svg file under outputs, or a mermaid code block / ASCII box diagram in explanation.md) expressing the billing segments or branches
Walks through one example with concrete dates, clearly stating which segments are charged and which are free because they are covered by the active plan
Covers the 'activation time precedes subscription start' case, explaining that the gap between activation and subscription start must be billed separately
Mentions that with multiple plans (plansList.size() > 1) it carves out a separate billing segment for the gaps between adjacent plans
Translates magic values such as feePayFlag="T"/no-payment-required and status into business meaning, and mentions the early return and any side effects when no charge is required
Explains business logic rather than line-by-line syntax; output keeps code identifiers verbatim

evals.prompt: What exactly does the validateRule method in src/pricing/RuleValidationService.java check? Especially the regulated-price (regulatedPrice) part — help me make the business rules clear.
evals.expected_output: Explains the two kinds of validation in validateRule: (1) when the pricing type is regulatedPrice and isCustom=false (not a tenant-custom rule), it looks up the corresponding regulated rule (isCustom=true) and compares unit charge timePrice, daily cap highPrice, and free duration freeTime one by one, rejecting with the matching message on any mismatch; if no regulated rule is found, it lets the request through. (2) Uniqueness validation for the same tenantId+itemType pricing rule: reject on add if one already exists; reject on update if one exists and it is not the current record. Pairs a decision table/flowchart, gives a concrete example that gets rejected, and explains the meanings of values like regulatedPrice and the true/false of isCustom.
expectations: Produces at least one diagram (.svg file, or mermaid/ASCII) expressing the validation branches or decision table
Distinguishes and explains the two kinds of validation: regulated-price consistency comparison (timePrice/highPrice/freeTime) and per-item-type rule uniqueness

States that the precondition for the regulated-price comparison is chargeType=regulatedPrice and isCustom=false, and that it passes straight through when no corresponding regulated rule (isCustom=true) is found
Explains how the uniqueness validation decides differently under the add vs update types (excluding the current ruleId on update)
Gives a concrete example that triggers one of the rejection messages (e.g. an input unit price that does not match the regulated price)
Translates magic values like regulatedPrice and the true/false of isCustom into business meaning; explains business logic rather than line-by-line syntax

evals.prompt: Walk me through the updateOrderWhenPaySuccess payment-success callback in src/order/OrderService.java. The handling seems to differ across order types (top-up / subscription / standard) and item categories — help me untangle it.
evals.expected_output: Lays out how this payment callback branches by order type: top-up (change settlement/relationship channel and notify), subscription (mark as paid, received = billed), historical order (dataSource=legacy), and finally the standard order; within standard it branches further by item category STANDARD/PREMIUM and 'whether already completed' — completed sets PAID, not yet completed (paid in advance) sets BILLING to keep accruing. Explains the amount math: total paid = previous paid + this payment, outstanding = due − total paid. Pairs a flowchart/decision table, gives an example with concrete amounts, and translates status magic values like PAID/BILLING.
expectations: Produces at least one diagram (.svg file, or mermaid/ASCII) expressing the branch structure by order type / item category
Explains the branching by order type: how top-up, subscription, historical order (legacy), and standard order are each handled
Explains the further branching within standard orders by item category STANDARD/PREMIUM and 'whether already completed', and the corresponding order-status changes
Explains the amount math: total paid = previous paid + this payment, outstanding = due − total paid
Gives a walkthrough example with concrete amounts (due / paid / this payment / outstanding)
Translates status magic values like PAID/BILLING and the order-type values into business meaning; explains business logic rather than line-by-line syntax
